retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Post-rotational nystagmus: reflexive horizontal nystagmus that occurs with a rapid brake to a unidirectional head rotation, with a contralateral fast phase and a subjective rocking boat sensation

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Per-rotational nystagmus: horizontal jerk nystagmus that occurs with sustained head rotations, with fast phases ipsilateral to the rotation

retrieved_docs.title: Trendelenburg Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Prerequisites of Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The patient can stand on the affected side for more than 30 seconds The patient can understand commands The patient has intact coordination The patient has free abduction movement; no fixed adduction deformity

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vestibular ocular reflex: reflex controlled by semicircular canals in the inner ear,  seen when the patient sees a fixed image, and a rapid head rotation is elicited bilaterally.

retrieved_docs.title: Caloric Reflex Test -- Definition/Introduction -- Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: An interaural variability in the caloric paresis of more than 22 to 25% and the directional preponderance greater than 26 to 30% indicates an asymmetric response. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Jackson Cross Cylinder -- Clinical Significance -- Determining/Refining the Astigmatic Axis
retrieved_docs.content: The degree of rotation while refining the axis is dependent on the power of the cylinder and the quality of response from the patient. After the initial axis change, the degree of subsequent axis changes is dependent on the magnitude of the initial change and the quality of the response.

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction -- Strength
retrieved_docs.content: To evaluate strength, the Medical Research Council scale of muscle strength (MCR-scale) is commonly used that grades the strength into 0 to 5 [4] : 0 – No contraction 1 – Flicker or trace of contraction 2 – Full range of active movement, with gravity eliminated 3 – Active movement against gravity 4 – Active movement against gravity and resistance 5 – Normal power

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Physiologic end-point nystagmus: horizontal jerks nystagmus results from testing oculomotor movements too far laterally

retrieved_docs.title: Jackson Cross Cylinder -- Introduction -- Astigmatism
retrieved_docs.content: To understand the JCC and its principle, it is important to take a brief look at the concept of astigmatism. [6] The word " astigmatism " comes from a combination of the English article " a, " which in this case means "without," and the greek word " stigma ," which means "point." In combining the words, the definition indicates the concept of "without a point."

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Optokinetic after-nystagmus: persisting ipsilateral optokinetic nystagmus after the visual stimulus has stopped can persist for seconds and dissipate.

retrieved_docs.title: Trendelenburg Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Gait Test
retrieved_docs.content: The examiner asks the patient to walk a short distance. In a normal gait, the body shifts the weight to the stance leg, allowing the center of gravity to shift, stabilizing the body. In a patient with a positive Trendelenburg sign constituting abductor weakness, when they lift the unaffected leg, the shift does not occur; therefore, the patient is unable to maintain balance, leading to instability.

retrieved_docs.title: Beevor Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Beevor sign is characterized by abnormal cephalad movement of the umbilicus during truncal flexion while a patient is supine. [1] In individuals with normal muscle function, the rectus abdominis contracts as a single unit without a predominance of the upper over the lower portion or the left over the right side. [2] Consequently, the navel remains stationary during trunk flexion.

retrieved_docs.title: Right Ventricular Hypertrophy -- Introduction -- Physiology of Right Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Preload, afterload, and intrinsic contractility abnormalities can cause ventricular dysfunction. Muscle fiber orientation variations in the right ventricle determine its symmetrical shortening in the longitudinal and radial planes. Longitudinal shortening contributes significantly more to ejection than in the left ventricle. The pronounced right ventricle shortening along the longitudinal axis facilitates systolic function measurement using straightforward techniques that avoid geometric assumptions or meticulous endocardial definition, addressing limitations in noninvasive assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Observation begins with accessing any visible gross abnormalities of skin and other components of the musculoskeletal system. Palpation uses from light to firm pressure to identify and quantify the abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system, pain/tenderness, trigger points. Normal or abnormal findings that could be elicited by observation and palpation include: symmetry/asymmetry - skin color and appearance, rash, ulcers, lack of sweating hair abnormalities - warmth and heat - Swelling including effusions, nodules, and inflammatory findings like synovial and periarticular thickening - muscle atrophy, tone, contractures, and spasms - crepitations - Joint deformities including spine like kyphosis and scoliosis. Manipulation consists of different techniques to access the range of motion (ROM), strength, sensations, reflexes, and gait. The proper evaluation consists mainly of testing strength (evaluate individually the muscle capacity and integrity), range of motion (evaluate the joint independently, it's restrictions, and hypo or hypermobility), reflex and sensory function (evaluate dermatomes, reflex and sensory function, to identify possibles correlations and dysfunctions between musculoskeletal and neural system), gait analysis (evaluate the integrated functions of locomotion), and trigger points (to evaluate myofascial pain, presence of trigger points and association with patient symptoms).

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Optokinetic or pendular nystagmus: multi-direction (eg vertical, torsional, or horizontal) nystagmus in response to moving or rotating visual fields or objects, the slow phase is ipsilateral to the visual stimuli, and it does not have a fast phase. [2] Asymmetry is abnormal, and the lesion can localize to the parietal-occipital cortex.

retrieved_docs.title: Modified Ashworth Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The assessment begins with the joint in either a fully flexed or fully extended position, depending on the muscle group being tested. The examiner should passively move the limb through its full range of motion at a controlled speed, ideally completing the movement within approximately 1 second. The movement should be performed smoothly and without excessive force.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2783
retrieved_docs.content: Physical therapy exercises; manipulation; and other procedures are identified as a distinct category that classifies itself, indicating that these therapeutic interventions are grouped together under a single classification for clinical and therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 172

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, and crushing injury or internal injury is in turn associated with the Arabic term “إصابة,” which means injury; thus, when a condition is described as “due to” a crushing or internal injury, it denotes a direct causal link to an injury.
